If it were a real management scenario, I wouldn't use a tool like this unless mandated to do so. These things are probably very appealing for certain types of managers, but not to me. They don't tell me anything about employees' needs and goals, what might be causing differences in "performance", and how it is that I could lift them up. Instead the intention with things like this is to identify supposed underperformers, according to the metrics that developers of such systems think are important or which can be tracked and calculated, in order to issue punitive forms of punishment.

I found the simulation realy interesting, especialy how it showed the tension between automated data and a manager's own judgement. While the report generated may give some general impressions / interpretations of how the team functions, I still beleive it is up to the manager to determine the performance score of the employee.  If the manager is engaged with his/her team, they should not require a system to determine how the employee is performing.  Based on tasks completed, deadlines met, on budget, etc, these are more accurate metrics of performance that a manager could use.  We have an employee that is regularly absent, does not complete tasks accurately or to the full degree necessary, and is constantly handing in tasks late.  The manager does not need a system to identify the performance of this individual.
